blocks.content:
A **string** is text data. Any text you want to work with in JavaScript is a string.

## Creating Strings

Strings must be wrapped in quotes (either single or double):

```javascript
let message = 'Hello, World!';       // single quotes
let name = "Alice";                  // double quotes
let question = `What's your name?`;  // backticks (template literals)
```

blocks.title: Working with Strings

blocks.content:
## String Methods

Strings have helpful built-in methods:

- `.length` - Get the number of characters
- `.toUpperCase()` - Convert to UPPERCASE
- `.toLowerCase()` - Convert to lowercase
- `.includes('text')` - Check if string contains text
- `.slice(start, end)` - Get part of the string

blocks.title: String Methods
blocks.code:
let word = 'JavaScript';

console.log(word.length);           // 10
console.log(word.toUpperCase());    // JAVASCRIPT
console.log(word.toLowerCase());    // javascript
console.log(word.includes('Script')); // true
console.log(word.slice(0, 4));      // Java

blocks.instruction: Get the length of the string 'Hello':
blocks.explanation: The .length property gives us the number of characters in a string.

questions.question: How do you create a string in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Strings must be wrapped in single quotes ('), double quotes ("), or backticks (`).

questions.question: What does 'Hello'.toUpperCase() return?
questions.explanation: toUpperCase() converts all characters to uppercase letters.
